prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 42-year-old woman.
I read online that Bell's palsy almost always goes away completely on its own within a few weeks. My cousin had it and didn't take any medication and was fine.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I woke up this morning and noticed the right side of my face feels weak. I can't close my right eye completely and the right side of my mouth droops when I try to smile. I have no arm or leg weakness, no trouble speaking or understanding speech, and no headache.
- History: I had a mild cold about a week ago. No diabetes. No history of stroke or heart disease.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden one-sided weakness that includes inability to close the right eye and mouth droop, with no arm/leg weakness or speech problems and a recent cold, most strongly fits a peripheral facial nerve palsy (commonly called Bell’s palsy). That is different from a stroke, which usually causes other limb or speech changes and often spares forehead movement. Early treatment (usually oral steroids) is most helpful if started soon, and eye protection is important because you cannot fully close the eye. If new symptoms like arm/leg weakness, trouble speaking, numbness, severe headache, or vision changes appear, that would require immediate attention.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden inability to close the right eye and drooping of the whole right side of the face suggests a peripheral facial nerve problem (Bell’s palsy) rather than a stroke, because strokes usually spare the forehead and cause other arm/leg or language problems. A mild cold a week ago fits a post‑viral cause. Most people with Bell’s palsy improve, but treatment with oral steroids is most effective if started within about 72 hours. Protect the eye (lubricating drops, ointment, taping/patching at night) to avoid corneal injury. See a doctor promptly to confirm the diagnosis and discuss steroids. Go to the emergency room now if you develop weakness in an arm/leg, trouble speaking, severe headache, breathing difficulty, or worsening symptoms.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden right-sided inability to fully close the eye and mouth droop, with no arm/leg weakness or speech problems and a recent cold, most closely fits peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy). Many people recover, but early steroid treatment (ideally within 72 hours of onset) can improve chances of full recovery, and protecting the exposed eye with lubricating drops and patching is important to prevent corneal injury. You should be examined to confirm the diagnosis, rule out other causes (including stroke), and start appropriate treatment.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You most likely have an acute peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy): sudden weakness of the entire right side of the face (including inability to close the eye) commonly follows a recent cold. Because you have no arm/leg weakness, speech trouble, or headache, a stroke is less likely. Early treatment with oral corticosteroids (ideally within 72 hours) improves chances of full recovery, and protecting the eye (lubricating drops, patching) prevents corneal injury. You should still be evaluated to confirm the diagnosis, start treatment if appropriate, and rule out other causes.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You most likely have a peripheral facial nerve palsy (commonly called Bell’s palsy). Clues are sudden one-sided facial weakness with inability to close the eye and mouth droop, no arm/leg weakness or speech loss, and a recent cold. Many cases improve on their own, but starting corticosteroids within about 72 hours increases the chance of full recovery. Protect the affected eye (artificial tears by day, ointment and/or an eye patch at night) to avoid corneal injury. Less likely causes (stroke, Ramsay Hunt, ear problems) are possible but less consistent with your symptoms; they still need evaluation if new signs appear.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): The sudden right-sided facial weakness with inability to fully close your eye and mouth droop fits a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy), especially after a recent cold. Many people do recover, but early treatment with a short course of corticosteroids (ideally started within ~72 hours) improves the chance of full recovery. Eye care (artificial tears during the day, ointment/patch at night) is important to prevent corneal injury. Because you have no arm/leg weakness, speech problems, or severe headache, a stroke is less likely, but a prompt medical evaluation is needed to confirm the diagnosis, start treatment if appropriate, and rule out other causes.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Likely a peripheral facial-nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy). Sudden one-sided facial weakness after a recent cold, with inability to fully close the eye and a drooping mouth, fits that pattern. Many people recover, but starting oral steroids within about 72 hours improves the chance of a full recovery. Protect the affected eye from drying or injury (lubricating drops, ointment at night, or patching). You should be examined soon to confirm the diagnosis, discuss steroid treatment, and rule out other causes. Seek emergency care immediately only if you develop arm/leg weakness, numbness, slurred speech, severe headache, or breathing/swallowing problems.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden right-sided inability to close the eye and mouth droop fits a peripheral facial nerve palsy (commonly called Bell’s palsy), especially after a recent viral cold. Unlike a stroke, Bell’s palsy usually affects the whole half of the face including the forehead; you have no arm/leg weakness or speech problems, which makes stroke less likely. Early treatment with corticosteroids (ideally within 72 hours) can improve recovery, and eye care (lubricating drops, patching at night) is important to protect the cornea when you can’t fully close the eye. If you develop limb weakness, trouble speaking, severe headache, vision loss, or breathing problems, seek emergency care.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden inability to close the right eye and drooping of the right mouth fits a peripheral facial-nerve palsy (commonly called Bell’s palsy), which often follows a viral illness and causes one-sided facial weakness. Unlike a stroke, Bell’s palsy usually affects forehead movement and eye closure. Early treatment with oral corticosteroids (ideally started within ~72 hours) can improve recovery, and protecting the eye (artificial tears by day, taping or a patch at night) prevents corneal damage. Watch for new or worsening symptoms — limb weakness, numbness, slurred speech, severe headache, vision loss, or loss of consciousness — which would suggest something other than Bell’s palsy.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): This sounds most like Bell’s palsy — a sudden weakness of the facial nerve on one side that commonly follows a viral illness. The key clue is involvement of the forehead (you can’t fully close your eye) plus mouth droop, with no arm/leg weakness or speech trouble; that pattern favors a peripheral facial nerve problem over a stroke. Many cases improve on their own, but starting treatment early (usually oral corticosteroids) can improve recovery, and the eye needs protection if it can’t fully close to prevent dryness or injury.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%
